sections.text:
Thymus
The thymus is a specialized organ of the
immune system. The only known function of
the thymus is the production and
"education" of T-lymphocytes (T cells),
which are critical cells of the adaptive
immune system. Its epithelial cells provide a
framework containing T cells as well as
smaller numbers of other lymphoid cells.

sections.text:
The Endocrine Thymus
Generally speaking, the thymic hormones are all modulators of T-cell differentiation acting at
different stages: they showed immune-stimulating effects in a variety of clinical trials (Bach
1983; Hadden 1993) and they modulate T-helper and T-cytotoxic functions.
However, a number of thymic hormones seem to interact with the neuroendocrine system,
And an emerging core of information is identifying the thymic hormone thymulin as a key link
between the thymus and the anterior pituitary gland. In fact, many hormones exert a
stimulatory effect on thymulin serum levels (PRL, IGF-1, GH, glucocorticoid hormones,
estradiol, progesterone or testosterone, T3).
THYMOSIN Thymosin has displayed potent stimulatory effects on T-lymphocyte-mediated immunity. Thymosin
shows also a stimulating effect on gonadotropin releasing hormones. The release of another pituitary hormone
known as prolactin, as well as human growth hormone and adrenal corticotropin (ACTH) are increased by (in vitro)
thymosin administration. THYMULIN It affects the differentiation of immature bone marrow cells in the function
of T-lymphocytes. The serum level of thymulin decreases with age, and it coincides with thymus atrophy. Thymulin
requires zinc for full biological activity
THYMIC HUMORAL FACTOR (THF) Peripheral blood obtained from patients with chronic hepatitis B and viral
infections responded to THF with increased production of IL-2, a protein produced by lymphocytes. This suggests a
possible antiviral role for this thymic hormone.
THYMOPOIETIN It thoroughly enhances T-lymphocyte differentiation and the effect of function on mature T-

sections.text:
The Immune System
The Innate and Acquired Immunity
The vertebrate immune response is a highly specific defense system, which
distinguishes self from non-self and thereby protects the organism from extern factors
such as infectious agents (bacteria, viruses, fungi and parasites) and toxins. The
immune system consists of two main compartments, the humoral immunity that
leads to the synthesis of circulating antibody molecules by B-cells, and the cellular
immunity that culminates in activated T-cells. This differentiation has first been clearly
demonstrated by Cooper et al. in 1965.
The Humoral Immunity
Humoral immunity is provided by the plasma cell also called AFC (antibody forming
cell) as effector cell of the B-cell lineage. B-cells carry membrane-bound
immunoglobulins on their surface, the B-cell antigen receptors that have the same
binding specificity as the antibody finally produced by the mature AFC. After contact
with an appropriate antigen, they produce and secrete large amounts of circulating
antibodies/immunoglobulins that recognize and neutralize free antigen in serum and
tissue fluids. These immunocomplexes activate the complement system and facilitate
phagocytosis by opsonization.
The Cellular Immunity
It is supported by T-cells

sections.text:
All cells of the immune system arise
from the same pluripotent stem
cells through two main lines of
differentiation: the lymphoid
lineage produces lymphocytes, and
the myeloid lineage produces
phagocytes and other cells.
The major lymphoid organs and
tissues are divided into primary
(central) and secondary (peripheral)
lymphoid organs.

sections.text:
The major lymphoid organs and tissues are divided into primary (central) and secondary
(peripheral) lymphoid organs.
Thymus and bone marrow are the mammals’ primary lymphoid organs where the lymphoid
cells acquire their repertoire of specific antigen receptors that enable them to meet the
innumerable antigenic challenges throughout life.
The thymus is the site where T-lymphocytes differentiate from lymphoid stem cells,
proliferate and mature into functional cells, whereas the B-lymphocytes develop and mature
in the bone marrow.
Briefly, lymphocytes develop in the primary organs, but migrate to and function in secondary
organs and tissues such as the spleen, lymph nodes and mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue
(MALT).

sections.text:
The development of the thymus is linked to that of the parathyroid glands
While the inferior parathyroid
glands derive from the dorsal part
of the third pharyngeal pouch, and
the thymus arises from the ventral
part of the third pharyngeal pouch.
The thymus appears in the form of
two flask-shaped entodermal
diverticula, and extends into the
surrounding mesoderm in front of
the ventral aortæ. Here they meet
and become joined to one another
by connective tissue, but there is
never any fusion of the thymus
tissue proper. The pharyngeal
opening of each diverticulum is soon
obliterated, but the neck of the flask
persists for some time as a cellular
cord.
As the inferior parathyroid glands
and the thymus migrate together
toward the mediastinum, then they
eventually separate. In most cases,
the inferior parathyroid glands
become localized near the inferior
poles of the thyroid, and the thymus
continues to migrate toward the
mediastinum.

sections.text:
The thymus is an encapsulated soft bi-lobed organ, the two parts being joined at the
midline by connective tissue that merges with the capsule of each lobe; they are
occasionally united, so as to form a single mass, and sometimes separated by an
intermediate lobe.

sections.text:
The thymus continues to grow until the
time of puberty and then begins to
atrophy.
It is relatively large in infancy (weighing 25
g at birth) reaching a maximal weight
in adolescence between 12 and 19
years (35 g), and gradually involutes with
age (between 20 and 60 years) with
progressive fatty replacement (15 g at 60
years of age).

sections.text:
HISTOLOGY
A thin capsule (connective tissue) surrounds each lobe and, in most species,
gives rise to septae, that partially subdivide the thymus into interconnecting
lobules of variable size and orientation.
It can be divided in:
Cortex. ‘Outer’ region of the thymus; supports early stage
lymphocytic development, including positive selection for
recognition of self-MHC (major histocompatibility complex).
Medulla. ‘Inner’ region of the thymus; location of later
stages of lymphocytic differentiation to CD4+ and CD8+
stages, including negative selection against tissue-specific
antigen recognition.

ocrObservations.text: Rashidi H MD, Nguyen J MD et al. HematologyOutlines.com

sections.text:
The thymus is involved in the production
of hormones that play a role in T-cell
differentiation.
T-lymphocytes are designated as such
because they are derived from or
influenced by the thymus hormone. To
become mature, all T-lymphocytes must
reside in the thymus gland for a period
of time.
The stem cells arriving from the bone
marrow first colonize the subcapsular
region (through the cortico-medullary
blood vessels) where they proliferate,
actively generating the thymocyte
population.
During the maturation period within the
thymus, T-lymphocytes eventually
become either CD4+ cells or CD8+ cells.
Only those thymocytes expressing CD4
or CD8 characteristics are positively
selected to reach to the lymphatic
system.
This differentiation process results in
mature lymphocytes that can recognize
foreign bodies, viruses or cancerous
cells, in the context of major
histocompatible complex hormones.

ocrObservations.text: C T cell development in the thymus

sections.text:
The bulk of the supporting framework in the
thymus is composed of the network of
epithelial-reticular cells. Epithelial cells in the
subcapsular region of the thymus form a layer
1 or 2 cells deep.
In the outer cortex and ensheathing blood
vessels, epithelial cells are thin and sheet-like,
but elsewhere they assume a stellate
appearance.
Epithelial cells are divided into distinct
populations that differ in antigenic expression,
ultrastructural characteristics and capacity to
synthesize the thymic hormones thymulin,
thymosin, thymopoetin and thymic humoral
factor.
Immunohistochemically, epithelial cells can be
divided into four distinct subtypes:
subcapsular, cortical, inner cortical, medullary
and Hassalls corpuscles.

sections.text:
Corticomedullary Junction
The corticomedullary junction is characterized by
plentiful blood vessels (predominantly
arterioles) with scant perivascular connective
tissue and mature and immature T lymphocytes.
Dendritic cells and variable numbers of
perivascular B-lymphocytes and plasma cells
occur in the corticomedullary zone.
B-cells and plasma cells increase with increasing
age.
Medulla
The medulla, is continuous between adjacent
lobules and can form small buds that reach deep
into the cortex that in some places reach the
capsule.
The medulla is paler staining, less densely
cellular than the cortex, and contains more
mature T-cells, prominent epithelial cells,
Hassalls corpuscles, macrophages, dendritic
cells (non-phagocytic, bone marrow-derived
cells), B lymphocytes and rarely myeloid cells.
The medullary T-lymphocytes are larger, paler-
staining and have more cytoplasm than cortical
lymphocytes.
Hassal's corpuscles can be detected in the medulla. These
are made up of flat non-secreting epithelial cells arranged in
a concentric layers that have keratinised. These structures
are only found in the thymus.

sections.text:
Thorax: The most part of the organ lies in the
superior mediastinum and anterior part of inferior
mediastinum.
It extends from the 4th costal cartilage upward, as
high as the lower border of the thyroid gland (left
lobe).
It is covered by the sternum, and by the origins of
the Sternohyoideus and Sternothyreoideus.
Below, it rests upon the pericardium, being
separated from the aortic arch and great vessels by a
layer of fascia.
Neck
The thymus is located in part in the inferior portion
of the anterior neck, where it lies on the front and
sides of the trachea, behind the Sternohyoidei and
Sternothyreoidei.

sections.text:
The thymus is visible in CT
or MRN axial sections just
anterior to the ascending
aorta and inferior to the left
brachiocephalic vein.
sections.title: Page 20

sections.text:
Blood supply:
•arterial supply from the inferior
thyroid and internal thoracic
arteries
•venous drainage from the thymus
end into the left brachiocephalic,
internal thoracic and inferior thyroid
veins.
Lymphatic drainage: parasternal,
brachiocephalic and
tracheobronchial lymph nodes
